title: Hybrid AI-Driven Computer-Aided Engineering Optimization: Large Language Models Versus Regression-Based Models Validated Through Finite-Element Analysis
inputData: FEA仿真数据（几何参数与力学响应）
contribution: 提出LLM（GPT-4o）与FEA结合的结构几何参数优化框架，仅需18个初始样本即可生成可行设计方案，数据效率远超传统回归模型（需756次FEA仿真），并展示跨材料泛化能力
abstract: This study investigates the application potential of large language models (LLMs), particularly GPT-4o, in generating geometric parameter suggestions during the early stages of structural design. Design recommendations from the LLM are validated using a finite-element solver (FFE Plus solver), forming the core workflow of the proposed approach. To assess its effectiveness, the LLM's performance is compared against traditional regression-based surrogate models, which serve as baseline references. A two-hole hanger bracket serves as the case study, evaluating prediction accuracy, data efficiency, generalization capability, and workflow complexity across three materials: 6061-T6, AISI 304, and AISI 1020. The key evaluation indicators include safety factor (SF) and Mass. The results show that the regression models offer high accuracy and interpretability but require extensive simulation data; in this study, each material required 252 samples to adequately cover the design space. In contrast, GPT-4o produced feasible design suggestions using only 18 initial samples, combining semantic prompting and finite-element analysis. Its prediction accuracy improved significantly with a small number of iterations, demonstrating superior data efficiency and cross-material adaptability.
methodology: The study proposes an integrated workflow combining FEA (SolidWorks Simulation 2021 with FFE Plus solver), second-order polynomial regression modeling, and LLM-based (GPT-4o) parameter recommendation. A two-hole hanger bracket is used as the case study with three geometric design parameters: thickness (Z), inner fillet radius (R), and rib angle (A). The regression model is trained on 252 FEA samples per material (756 total) using full factorial design. For the LLM approach, structured prompts are designed in five stages: material property declaration, boundary conditions specification, initial optimization request, search space refinement, and cross-material validation. Only 18 FEA samples for AISI 1020 are provided as initial reference to GPT-4o; for the other two materials (6061-T6, AISI 304), only material properties are given. All LLM-generated parameter sets are validated through FEA to assess structural feasibility and prediction error.
results: For AISI 1020, the best LLM recommendation (Sample C28) achieved mass prediction error of -0.58% and SF error of 8.6% compared to FEA. For 6061-T6 (Sample C33), mass error was 0.2% and SF error 3%. For AISI 304 (Sample C35), mass error was 0.7% and SF error -3%. The regression models achieved R-squared = 1.000 for all materials but required 252 samples per material. In contrast, the LLM-based workflow required only about 27 FEA simulations across all three materials versus 756 for the regression models, demonstrating a substantial advantage in data efficiency. The regression model also failed to meet the SF > 3 constraint for 6061-T6, while GPT-4o consistently satisfied safety requirements across all materials. The LLM showed decreasing iteration requirements across materials (3 rounds for AISI 1020, 4 for 6061-T6, 2 for AISI 304), indicating learning transfer through prompt refinement.

inputDataEn: FEA simulation data (geometric parameters and mechanical responses)
